candidates.de_silos: Connects USDA/agriculture (an island vertical) to Ripple's NAME/ADDRESS/ZIP/FIPS carriers and, via LLC recipient names, to corporate registries and parcel data. Recipient address clustering reveals farm entities pooling subsidies through related shells.
candidates.access: EWG: web search/browse, no public bulk API (Apify scraper exists as a third-party route). USDA FSA: official flat files downloadable + FOIA Name/Address files. Free.
candidates.volume: Tens of millions of payment records since 1995
candidates.creative_join: Join EWG recipient address to Regrid/parcel owner-mailing-address: which subsidized farm entities also hold land elsewhere under the same address? Bridges who-gets-federal-money to who-owns-the-dirt via the ADDRESS key.

verdict.name: EWG Farm Subsidy Database + USDA FSA Name/Address Payment Files
verdict.access_reality: Split reality. EWG (farm.ewg.org): public web search/browse only — NO official public API or bulk download from EWG; only a third-party Apify scraper exists (~2.5M recipients, 1995-2024, name/location/payment). USDA FSA: the OFFICIAL public bulk route — Farm Payment Data flat files free at the FSA FOIA Electronic Reading Room (frequently-requested/payment-files); pre-2008 needs a FOIA request. FSA payment files carry recipient NAME + ADDRESS. Claimed 'tens of millions of records since 1995' is plausible (EWG cites ~2.5M recipients, far more individual payment rows).
verdict.notes: The DESTINATION is sound (recipient NAME+ADDRESS de-silos the USDA/ag island into the NAME/ADDRESS/ZIP/FIPS carrier mass, and farm-LLC names resolve to registries + parcel data), but the SOURCE framing is off: load the USDA FSA official flat files, not EWG. EWG has no clean public bulk feed — treat it as a UI/validation reference, not the load target. carries_claimed_key marked 'likely' because FSA flat files reliably carry name/address but exact ADDRESS granularity/redaction in the public flat files should be confirmed against an actual downloaded file before building the address-clustering join.

candidates.name: BLM Mineral & Land Records System (MLRS, ex-LR2000) — federal leases & mining claims
candidates.bridges: Federal oil/gas/geothermal leases, mining claims, and rights-of-way keyed by BLM serial number, with claimant/lessee NAME + ADDRESS and PLSS/legal-land-description geometry. Claimant names are frequently LLCs/operators.
candidates.de_silos: Brings federal public-land tenure (an island domain) into Ripple's NAME/ADDRESS/geo carriers. Lessee/claimant LLC names resolve to corporate registries and to USASpending/UEI contractor data; PLSS converts to lat/lon for spatial join.
candidates.access: BLM Reporting Application: configurable report exports (CSV), no auth, free. Bulk-ish via report downloads; some case types also on data.gov. Schema varies by case type.
candidates.volume: Millions of authorizations/claims across western states
candidates.creative_join: Cross BLM lessee/claimant NAME+ADDRESS against corporate registries and federal-contractor (UEI/DUNS) data: which entities holding cheap federal mineral leases also win federal contracts or share an address with energy shells? Ties public-land extraction to the corporate-ID backbone via NAME/ADDRESS.

clusters.thesis: universal entity hubs + the missing FIPS geo backbone (the connective glue)
clusters.bridge_thesis: Ripple's hard-ID backbone is single-vertical (healthcare NPI/CCN) and its 82 island datasets carry rich IDs (IMO/LEI/CIK/ADDRESS/ZIP) that touch nothing — a handful of universal-connector files (Wikidata's identifier-property graph, GLEIF's LEI crosswalks, and the Census/HUD geo backbone) wire those islands together by translating one ID type into another and turning coarse ZIP/ADDRESS into the universal FIPS geo key.

candidates.name: Wikidata external-identifier crosswalk (full JSON dump + SPARQL)
candidates.bridges: One Wikidata QID stitches together ~7,000 external-ID properties on the same entity at once: P5531=CIK, P1278=LEI, P458=IMO, P3083=ICAO airline, P229=IATA, NPI, GRID, ISNI, VIAF, etc. — so it is a single free file that maps CIK<->LEI<->IMO<->name<->country for any entity that exists in both systems.
candidates.de_silos: The mega-Rosetta-Stone for the whole warehouse: links the CIK island (SEC EDGAR, currently CIK=1 edge) to the LEI normalizer (zero LEI data), to the IMO island (NOAA AIS 7.3M rows + the 2 IMO edges), to FAA/OpenSky aircraft, and to healthcare NPI — translating across ID TYPES rather than matching same-key.
candidates.access: Free bulk download (~130 GB bz2 JSON, weekly) OR live SPARQL at https://query.wikidata.org/ (no auth, rate-limited). License CC0 (public domain). Recommend: SPARQL-extract only the identifier-property claims you carry (CIK/LEI/IMO/NPI/ICAO/CUSIP) into a thin crosswalk table rather than landing 130 GB.
candidates.volume: ~115M entities, ~24M external-ID claims (~35% of all claims); thin crosswalk extract is a few hundred MB
candidates.creative_join: A vessel in NOAA AIS (IMO) that is owned by a public company resolves via Wikidata to that company's CIK and LEI — connecting maritime, SEC filings, and GLEIF ownership through one free hop. No same-key match could ever do this.

verdict.name: Wikidata external-identifier crosswalk (full JSON dump + SPARQL)
verdict.access_reality: Confirmed real and CC0/public domain. Bulk dump at dumps.wikimedia.org/wikidatawiki/entities/latest-all.json.bz2 (~100-140 GB bz2, weekly). Live SPARQL at query.wikidata.org, no auth. IMPORTANT GOTCHA: the public SPARQL endpoint enforces a 60s query timeout and aggressive rate limits/User-Agent policy -- a broad 'pull all CIK+LEI+IMO claims' query will time out; you must paginate/chunk by property or use the WDQS dump. The 'extract only the ID properties' approach is the correct one. Property IDs verified: P5531=CIK, P1278=LEI, P458=IMO ship number, P229=IATA / P230=ICAO airline (candidate wrongly labels ICAO as P3083 -- minor), P1297=EIN. Coverage is uneven: corporate/maritime/aviation IDs are well-populated; NPI, DUNS, CUSIP, ICAO24, TAIL are sparse-to-absent on Wikidata.
verdict.notes: Genuine mega-Rosetta-Stone and the single highest-leverage de-silo candidate -- it translates ACROSS id types (CIK<->LEI<->IMO<->company name), exactly the warehouse's gap, and wires the CIK island (1 edge), the empty LEI normalizer, and the IMO island together. BUT the keys_carried list is over-claimed: confirm CIK/LEI/IMO/EIN/NAME/COUNTRY (good); treat NPI/DUNS/CUSIP/ICAO24/TAIL/PATENT as thin or unconfirmed -- do not assume healthcare NPI bridges here. Coverage is also opt-in (only entities notable enough to have a QID), so it's a high-value but incomplete crosswalk, not exhaustive.

candidates.name: GLEIF LEI mapping files (ISIN / BIC / MIC / OpenCorporates / S&P CIQ to LEI) + Golden Copy + Level-2 ownership
candidates.bridges: Each GLEIF relationship file is a pure 2-hard-ID crosswalk: ISIN-to-LEI (daily), BIC-to-LEI (monthly), MIC-to-LEI, OpenCorporates-ID-to-LEI (bi-weekly), S&P CIQ-to-LEI. ISIN embeds CUSIP for US securities, so ISIN-to-LEI effectively gives CUSIP<->LEI. Level-2 file is LEI<->LEI parent/ultimate-owner ('who owns whom').
candidates.de_silos: Activates Ripple's existing-but-empty LEI normalizer (0 LEI edges today) and wires it to the financial islands: Treasury debt/rates, FDIC enforcement/failed-banks, SEC EDGAR. Level-2 ownership turns flat entity lists into a corporate-control graph. Pairs with Wikidata to bridge LEI<->CIK.
candidates.access: Free open data, CC0/CC-BY, no auth. Concatenated/Golden-Copy at https://www.gleif.org/en/lei-data/gleif-golden-copy/download-the-golden-copy and per-mapping CSVs (e.g. https://www.gleif.org/en/lei-data/lei-mapping/download-isin-to-lei-relationship-files). CSV format, daily-to-monthly cadence.
candidates.volume: ~2.7M LEIs; ISIN-to-LEI tens of millions of rows; Level-2 hundreds of thousands of parent links
candidates.creative_join: A bond CUSIP in a Ripple finance dataset -> ISIN -> LEI -> Level-2 ultimate parent LEI -> Wikidata -> parent's CIK and SEC filings: a full ownership chain assembled from free crosswalk files, no entity-resolution guesswork.

verdict.name: GLEIF LEI mapping files (ISIN / BIC / MIC / OpenCorporates / S&P CIQ to LEI) + Golden Copy + Level-2 ownership
verdict.access_reality: Fully confirmed, free, no auth, open licence (CC0). Verified live: ISIN-to-LEI (daily), BIC-to-LEI (monthly), OpenCorporates-ID-to-LEI (bi-weekly) all have dedicated download pages under gleif.org/en/lei-data/lei-mapping. Golden Copy + Level-2 (RR-CDF) downloadable with no registration; Level-2 carries IS_DIRECTLY/IS_ULTIMATELY_CONSOLIDATED_BY parent links. ISIN embeds CUSIP for US securities so ISIN->LEI effectively yields CUSIP<->LEI (confirmed by GLEIF/CUSIP Global Services). One number caveat: ISIN-to-LEI is ~10M ISINs linked to ~64k LEIs, not 'tens of millions of rows' against all 2.7M LEIs -- so the LEI<->security crosswalk is real but skewed toward securities-issuing entities.
verdict.notes: Pure 2-hard-ID crosswalk files -- the cleanest de-silo asset in this cluster. Directly activates the warehouse's existing-but-empty LEI normalizer and pairs with Wikidata for LEI<->CIK. Level-2 turns flat lists into an ownership graph. No entity-resolution guesswork. Strong recommend.

candidates.name: U.S. Census Geocoding Services API (address -> FIPS GEOID, state/county/tract/block)
candidates.bridges: Converts a raw ADDRESS into the full 15-digit FIPS GEOID (state+county+tract+block) plus lat/lon. This is the manufacturing machine for the join key Ripple is starved of: it stamps FIPS onto any of the 2,723 address-carrying edges that currently lack geo.
candidates.de_silos: Bridges the huge ADDRESS soft-key surface (422 carriers, 2,723 edges) to the universal US FIPS backbone (only 31 datasets). Every address-bearing island — FDIC, USASpending subawards, corporate registries — gains county/tract once geocoded, joining to ACS demographics and everything FIPS-keyed.
candidates.access: Free, no auth/no key. Batch mode up to 10,000 addresses/file (CSV); single-address REST returns JSON. MAF/TIGER benchmark. Rate-limited but generous; Python wrapper `censusgeocode` exists.
candidates.volume: On-demand; you geocode your own address rows (batch 10k at a time)
candidates.creative_join: Geocode the registered-agent / principal-office address on a corporate filing and a federal contractor's SAM address to the same census BLOCK — surfacing entities that share a physical location (shell-company / co-location signal) that no shared ID would reveal.

verdict.name: U.S. Census Geocoding Services API (address -> FIPS GEOID, state/county/tract/block)
verdict.access_reality: Confirmed live and free, no API key. Returns lat/lon + state/county/tract/block FIPS via the 'geographies' return type. Batch limit confirmed at 10,000 records/file. US/PR/Island-areas only (not international -- pairs with GeoNames for the global side). REAL GOTCHAS to plan for: the batch endpoint is slow and frequently times out / 500s on large jobs, match rates on messy/PO-box/rural addresses are mediocre, and there's an informal throttle -- you'll want retry logic and to chunk well under 10k. Python wrapper censusgeocode exists.
verdict.notes: The manufacturing machine for FIPS -- promotes the 2,723 address-carrying edges to the universal geo backbone. Co-location/shell-company creative join (same census block from two filings) is legitimate and novel. Caveat the reliability/throughput, not the existence.

candidates.name: HUD-USPS ZIP Code Crosswalk Files (ZIP <-> tract / county FIPS / CBSA / CD)
candidates.publisher: U.S. Dept. of Housing & Urban Development (PD&R)
candidates.bridges: Twelve quarterly crosswalk files mapping ZIP <-> census tract, ZIP <-> county FIPS, ZIP <-> CBSA, ZIP <-> congressional district (both directions), each row carrying allocation ratios (residential/business/other) for weighted apportionment.
candidates.de_silos: Directly fixes Ripple's biggest geo asymmetry: 5,541 ZIP edges / 368 ZIP carriers but FIPS on only 31 datasets. This file promotes every ZIP-keyed island to county/tract FIPS, joining them to ACS, TIGER, and each other on the universal geo key — no geocoding round-trip needed.
candidates.access: Free. API at https://www.huduser.gov/hudapi/public/usps (token via free account) returns JSON; also Excel/CSV bulk download. Quarterly cadence. Twelve `type=1..12` crosswalk variants.
candidates.volume: ~40k ZIPs x multiple geographies per quarter; ~hundreds of thousands of rows per file
candidates.creative_join: A dataset that only knows business ZIP (e.g. an enforcement list) gets allocated to congressional district by the BUSINESS-ratio column — letting you join corporate misconduct to the specific representative whose district it sits in, weighted by business density.

verdict.name: HUD-USPS ZIP Code Crosswalk Files (ZIP <-> tract / county FIPS / CBSA / CD)
verdict.access_reality: Confirmed. 12 quarterly files (6 ZIP->geo, 6 geo->ZIP) covering tract, county, county-subdivision, CBSA, CBSA-division, congressional district, each with res_ratio/bus_ratio/oth_ratio/tot_ratio allocation columns. Two access paths: free Excel/CSV bulk download, AND a REST API at huduser.gov/hudapi/public/usps that DOES require a free account + Bearer token (Authorization: Bearer header). Quarterly cadence confirmed. So 'no auth' is only true for the bulk files -- the API path is gated behind a free token.
verdict.notes: Fixes the warehouse's biggest geo asymmetry (5,541 ZIP edges vs FIPS on 31 datasets) without a geocoding round-trip. The bus_ratio->congressional-district creative join (corporate misconduct to a specific representative) is sound. Only caveat: token needed for the API; use bulk CSV if you want zero auth.

candidates.name: Census TIGER/Line + ACS 5-year via Census Data API (the FIPS demographic backbone)
candidates.bridges: TIGER/Line gives the authoritative geometry + GEOID for every state/county/tract/block/place/CD; the ACS API attaches thousands of demographic/economic variables keyed on the same FIPS GEOID. Together they are the canonical lookup that every FIPS in the warehouse resolves against (including County Business Patterns, which carries NAICS x FIPS).
candidates.de_silos: Becomes the hub the geo backbone hangs off: once HUD/Census-geocoder stamp FIPS onto the ZIP and ADDRESS islands, TIGER/ACS gives every one of them population, income, and industry context — turning 31 lonely FIPS datasets into a dense, demographically-enriched geo graph that joins to NAICS (6,441 edges) via County Business Patterns.
candidates.access: Free; ACS API needs a free key, TIGER shapefiles need none. JSON (API) + shapefile/GDB bulk at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/. Annual cadence.
candidates.volume: ~11M TIGER features; ACS = thousands of variables x ~85k tracts
candidates.creative_join: Join NAICS-keyed business datasets to County Business Patterns (NAICS x county FIPS) to ground 'same industry code' soft joins in real establishment counts per county — converting Ripple's coarsest key (NAICS, 6,441 edges) into a quantified, geo-anchored signal.

verdict.name: Census TIGER/Line + ACS 5-year via Census Data API (the FIPS demographic backbone)
verdict.access_reality: data.json discovery endpoint is valid (project-open-data v1.1 JSON-LD; lists CBP/ZBP/PEP/nonemp etc.). ACS 5-year is a standard, long-established Census API dataset accessed by FIPS geography hierarchy -- it wasn't in the truncated data.json excerpt I fetched but it is unquestionably part of the API. ACS API requires a free key (api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html); TIGER/Line shapefiles at www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/ need no key. Annual cadence. County Business Patterns carries NAICS x county FIPS as claimed.
verdict.notes: Marked scouted_not_loaded, consistent with warehouse state -- this is the FIPS demographic hub the geo backbone hangs off, not a novel island-breaker by itself. Real value is as the enrichment target once HUD/geocoder stamp FIPS. The CBP NAICS-x-FIPS join to ground the coarse NAICS key (6,441 edges) in real establishment counts is legit. Medium priority is right: it's infrastructure, not a Rosetta Stone.

candidates.name: GeoNames global gazetteer (allCountries.zip + alternateNames + admin codes)
candidates.bridges: 12M+ place features each with geonameid, lat/lon (WGS84), ISO country code, admin1 (carries FIPS-style codes), admin2, and a comma-separated alternateNames list (multilingual + historical names). It is the place-NAME <-> lat/lon <-> country <-> admin-code reconciler for the whole non-US/international side.
candidates.de_silos: Wires the international islands (Swiss Zefix, Spanish BORME, Greek GEMI, Irish CRO foreign registries) and the NOAA AIS port-call data to a common COUNTRY+LATLON spine — Ripple's domestic geo glue (Census) stops at the US border; GeoNames extends the geo backbone globally and reconciles messy place NAMEs across languages.
candidates.access: Free, CC-BY 4.0, no auth. TSV bulk dumps + REST/JSON webservice (free account). Daily extract.
candidates.volume: 12M+ features; allCountries.zip ~350 MB compressed; alternateNamesV2 ~30M rows
candidates.creative_join: Resolve a foreign company's registered city NAME (from BORME/GEMI/CRO) plus a vessel's AIS port-call coordinate to the same GeoNames feature — joining a maritime movement to the corporate registry of the port's jurisdiction, a cross-domain link impossible without a global gazetteer.

verdict.name: GeoNames global gazetteer (allCountries.zip + alternateNames + admin codes)
verdict.access_reality: Confirmed live and free, CC-BY 4.0. allCountries.zip (~400MB, observed last-updated 2026-06-27, daily refresh) carries geonameid, lat/lon (WGS84), ISO country code, admin1-4 codes. AlternateNames are a SEPARATE download (alternateNamesV2.zip), not bundled in allCountries -- minor correction to the candidate. REST/JSON webservice exists but needs a free username and has daily credit limits; the bulk dumps are the unthrottled path. Caveat on the 'FIPS' claim: admin1 codes are ISO/GeoNames admin codes, and US admin2 maps to county FIPS, but non-US admin codes are NOT US-FIPS -- 'carries FIPS-style codes' is loose; treat as geo/admin reconciler, not a FIPS source outside the US.
verdict.notes: Extends the geo backbone past the US border -- wires the international corporate-registry islands (Zefix/BORME/GEMI/CRO) and NOAA AIS port calls to a common COUNTRY+LATLON+place-NAME spine. The vessel-port-call-to-registry-jurisdiction creative join is plausible and genuinely cross-domain. Solid, but it's a place reconciler (fuzzy NAME matching), not a hard-ID crosswalk -- medium priority is correct.

candidates.name: Wikidata SPARQL identifier-property catalog (the meta-list of all crosswalkable IDs)
candidates.bridges: A single SPARQL query (filter datatype=external-id) enumerates all ~7,000 external-ID properties and their formatter URLs — a living index of EVERY ID system Wikidata can translate between, including which of Ripple's exact keys (CIK P5531, LEI P1278, IMO P458, NPI P11102, FAA/ICAO) have property coverage and how densely populated each is.
candidates.de_silos: Operational scaffolding for the de-siloing program itself: tells you, before you build, which Ripple island IDs Wikidata can actually crosswalk and the population count per property — so you target the dense bridges (CIK/LEI/IMO) first instead of guessing. Feeds the tagger which new tokens (CUSIP, ICAO24, TAIL) are worth adding.
candidates.access: Free SPARQL endpoint, no auth, results as JSON/CSV. Same data also in the bulk dump. License CC0.
candidates.volume: ~7,000 identifier properties; query returns a few thousand rows
candidates.creative_join: Run one query to rank Wikidata's CIK/LEI/IMO/NPI properties by claim count and instantly know the maximum achievable bridge volume between Ripple's SEC, finance, maritime, and healthcare islands — turning crosswalk planning from speculation into a measured ROI list.

clusters.bridge_thesis: It shatters the 82-island enforcement silo by wiring "who got penalized" (FDIC/OCC/Fed bank actions, OSHA/WHD/NLRB labor cases, HHS-OIG/DOJ fraud, FSIS recalls) to "who got paid" (USASpending) and "who is banned" (SAM/LEIE) through EIN, UEI/CAGE, RSSD-CERT-charter crosswalks, and parent-company name/address rollups — converting coarse NAME/ADDRESS soft matches into hard-ID, publishable accountability joins.

candidates.name: Good Jobs First Violation Tracker (penalty aggregator + 3,000 parent-company crosswalk)
candidates.bridges: rolls 400+ federal/state/local enforcement agencies up to a single PARENT company ID, so an OSHA fine, an EPA penalty, a DOJ FCA settlement and a CFPB action against different legal-entity subsidiaries all resolve to ONE parent -- the canonical name/parent crosswalk that turns scattered NAME hits into one entity
candidates.de_silos: bridges every Ripple enforcement island at once: FDIC enforcement islands, EPA ECHO, OSHA/WHD, DOJ FCA settlements, NAAG multistate settlements, and FED_CFPB_COMPLAINTS -- the only source that already did the cross-agency parent dedup Ripple wants
candidates.access: web search free; CSV/XML bulk download is SUBSCRIBER-ONLY (paid tier, download caps by tier); no public API. Scrape of parent pages possible but ToS-limited
candidates.volume: ~600k+ penalty records, 3,000+ parent companies, 400+ agencies
candidates.creative_join: use it as a NAME->PARENT->NAICS resolver to collapse Ripple's 432 NAME-carrying datasets into deduped corporate families, THEN attach USASpending awards and SAM exclusions to the parent -- 'this conglomerate was fined by 6 agencies and still got $X in federal contracts'

verdict.name: Good Jobs First Violation Tracker (penalty aggregator + 3,000 parent-company crosswalk)
verdict.access_reality: Search/display is free on the web; bulk CSV/spreadsheet DOWNLOAD is SUBSCRIBER-ONLY. Tiers confirmed: $25/mo (1k records/search), $45/mo (5k), $150/mo (10k) -- and these are PER-SEARCH download caps, not full-dataset access. No public API. Full academic dataset only by emailing Philip Mattera. So the parent-crosswalk value is real but gated; you'd be scraping individual parent pages (ToS-limited) or paying.
verdict.notes: The dataset exists and the cross-agency parent rollup is genuinely the marquee feature -- 400+ agencies deduped to ~3,000 parent companies. BUT: (1) it is NOT free bulk; download is paywalled and even paid tiers cap at 10k records/search against a 600k+ record base, so reconstructing the full DB via subscription is painful. (2) The claimed EIN key is REFUTED -- VT's crosswalk key is PARENT_COMPANY NAME, not EIN. Public field set is Current Parent + Parent-at-penalty (both names), plus offense/penalty/agency/NAICS/location. It is a NAME->PARENT resolver, exactly as the 'creative_join' says, but do not expect a hard EIN. Treat the EIN entry in keys_carried as wrong.

candidates.name: SAM.gov Exclusions (debarment/suspension) Extract + API
candidates.bridges: single file carries UEI + CAGE + EIN/TIN + NPI on the SAME excluded-entity row -- a true multi-hard-ID Rosetta Stone: ties the federal-contractor key (UEI/CAGE) to the corporate/nonprofit key (EIN) to the healthcare key (NPI)
candidates.de_silos: wires the 'who is banned' surface to USASpending subawards (UEI), to the healthcare NPI/CCN backbone (NPI), and to any EIN-carrying source (IRS 990, CMS Open Payments); the UEI<->EIN<->NPI mapping alone connects three currently-separate Ripple clusters
candidates.access: free API key (register on SAM.gov); REST + CSV/JSON bulk extract; rate limits 10-10,000 req/day by account type; public exclusions need no login to view
candidates.volume: ~120k active exclusion records, daily refresh
candidates.creative_join: join LEIE-equivalent: 'excluded entity X (NPI) is the same legal entity as federal contractor Y (UEI/CAGE) and nonprofit Z (EIN)' -- catches debarred actors who reappear under a different ID type across domains

verdict.name: SAM.gov Exclusions (debarment/suspension) Extract + API
verdict.access_reality: Free with a SAM.gov registration + API key; public exclusions extract is the 'Public' sensitivity tier (no special clearance). REST API (api.sam.gov/entity-information/v4/exclusions, CSV or JSON) plus a daily public CSV bulk extract, also mirrored on data.gov. Rate limits tiered by account. UEI(ueiSAM)/CAGE/NPI confirmed present in the exclusion record layout.
verdict.notes: Strong de-silo candidate and already scouted. UEI + CAGE + NPI on the same excluded-entity row are CONFIRMED in the GSA extract layout -- that alone bridges federal-contractor space to the healthcare NPI backbone. BIG CAVEAT on EIN: the schema has a TIN field, but per 2 CFR 180 the TIN/SSN is only entered 'if available and may be disclosed under law' and is treated as PII -- it is effectively redacted/absent in the PUBLIC extract. So the headline 'true UEI<->EIN<->NPI Rosetta Stone' overstates it: you reliably get UEI<->CAGE<->NPI, NOT a populated EIN. Also NPI is only on the minority of healthcare-related exclusions. Real and valuable, but the EIN bridge is the weak/likely-empty leg.

candidates.name: FFIEC NPW / NIC Bulk Data Download (bank regulator ID crosswalk + structure)
candidates.bridges: the ATTRIBUTES extract crosswalks RSSD <-> FDIC certificate number <-> OCC charter number on one row, plus carries LEI; the RELATIONSHIPS table maps each bank to its bank-holding-company parent (RSSD->RSSD)
candidates.de_silos: this is the missing key that activates the FDIC enforcement/failed-banks ISLANDS -- FDIC actions key on CERT, OCC actions on charter, Fed actions on name; NPW joins all three to a parent RSSD, and the LEI field is a wide-open lever (Ripple has an LEI normalizer but ~0 LEI data)
candidates.access: free, no auth; bulk ZIP of CSVs (active + closed attributes, relationships, transformations); daily refresh. Note: ffiec.gov returns 403 to naive fetchers -- needs a real User-Agent header
candidates.volume: ~few hundred thousand entities (active+closed), full relationship/transformation history
candidates.creative_join: merge a bank's Fed/OCC/FDIC enforcement actions to its holding-company parent, then to the parent's CIK (public banks) and LEI -- 'this BHC's subsidiaries collected N enforcement orders across 3 regulators'; seeds the empty LEI backbone

verdict.name: FFIEC NPW / NIC Bulk Data Download (bank regulator ID crosswalk + structure)
verdict.access_reality: Free, no auth, bulk ZIP of CSVs (active + closed Attributes, Relationships, Transformations), daily refresh from ffiec.gov/npw/FinancialReport/DataDownload. Confirmed: ffiec.gov returns 403 to naive fetchers (both target URL and the data-dictionary PDF 403'd me) -- you MUST send a real browser User-Agent. Data dictionary confirms ID_RSSD, FDIC certificate, OCC charter, and LEI all live in the Attributes table.
verdict.notes: Best-in-cluster. Free, public, multi-hard-ID by design: Attributes crosswalks RSSD<->FDIC cert<->OCC charter and CARRIES LEI; the Relationships table maps each entity to its bank-holding-company parent (RSSD->RSSD). This is exactly the missing key to activate the FDIC enforcement/failed-banks ISLANDS (FDIC keys on cert, OCC on charter, Fed on RSSD) and it seeds the empty LEI backbone -- a genuine wide-open lever for Ripple. Only gotcha is the 403/User-Agent requirement, which is a header fix, not a wall.

candidates.name: labordata OSHA + WHD + NLRB enforcement (free SQLite mirrors)
candidates.publisher: Labor Data / bunkum.us (Forest Gregg, mirrors DOL/NLRB)
candidates.bridges: three labor-enforcement domains (OSHA 40.8M rows, WHD wage-theft 907k, NLRB unfair-labor 6M+) cleaned into one relational schema keyed on employer NAME+ADDRESS and case number, NAICS on OSHA establishments
candidates.de_silos: OSHA + WHD are already scouted as raw, but these are normalized free SQLite drops that bridge labor enforcement to EPA ECHO (shared establishment ADDRESS/NAICS) and to USASpending/SAM by employer NAME -- 'wage-theft firm also holds federal contracts'
candidates.access: free, public-domain CC0; nightly SQLite .db.zip via GitHub releases; also queryable online at labordata.bunkum.us
candidates.volume: OSHA 40.8M, WHD 907k, NLRB 6M+ (since 2010) plus historical CATS files
candidates.creative_join: establishment-level ADDRESS+NAICS join across OSHA<->EPA ECHO<->WHD to find single facilities cited by labor AND environmental regulators, then attach the parent via Violation Tracker

verdict.name: labordata OSHA + WHD + NLRB enforcement (free SQLite mirrors)
verdict.access_reality: Genuinely free and public-domain. labordata.bunkum.us hosts a Datasette warehouse (~8M rows, 281 tables: osha_enforcement, whisard/WHD, nlrb, lm10/20/30, opdr, crosswalks). Nightly SQLite .db.zip via GitHub releases under github.com/labordata. The specific nlrb.db.zip nightly-release URL pattern is real. CC0-ish public source data.
verdict.notes: Legit and useful. Keys are NAME + ADDRESS + NAICS + case number + ZIP (soft/coarse, as claimed -- no hard corporate ID, so joins are establishment-level address/NAICS, which matches Ripple's coarse-key reality). Value-add over the already-scouted raw OSHA/WHD is that it is pre-cleaned into one relational schema PLUS ships a union_names_crosswalk. Note heavy OVERLAP with the separate OLMS candidate (labordata already mirrors lm10/20/30 + opdr), so don't double-onboard the OLMS LM data twice. Verdict solid as a normalized labor-enforcement bridge; just right-size expectations to soft keys.

candidates.name: DOL OLMS Online Public Disclosure (LM-2/3/4 + LM-10/20/30) bulk yearly data
candidates.bridges: LM-2 union annual reports carry EIN + OLMS file number + address; LM-10 employer reports disclose payments to anti-union (persuader) consultants, naming BOTH the employer and the consultant firm -- a real EIN-carrying employer<->consultant relationship file
candidates.de_silos: EIN wires union/labor-org finances into Ripple's EIN backbone (IRS 990, the phantom-key EIN sources); LM-10/20 persuader links create an employer->consultant edge that exists nowhere else in the graph
candidates.access: free, no auth; 'Download Yearly Data' = ZIP of 46 pipe-delimited TXT files per year; documented in OLMS Guide PDF
candidates.volume: OLMS annual reports 16.7M+ rows; LM-10 166k, LM-20 57k, LM-30 65k filings
candidates.creative_join: EIN-join union LM-2 financials to IRS 990 (many unions/labor nonprofits file both), and use LM-10 to map which federal contractors (USASpending UEI via name) paid persuader firms during organizing drives

verdict.name: DOL OLMS Online Public Disclosure (LM-2/3/4 + LM-10/20/30) bulk yearly data
verdict.access_reality: Free, no auth. 'Download Yearly Data' (olmsapps.dol.gov/query/getYearlyData.do) returns a yearly ZIP of pipe-delimited TXT files (~46 files per year for LM-2/3/4); LM-10/20/30 persuader reports browsable since 2000 at the OPDR / persuader-reports.gov. Documented in the OLMS Guide PDF (olmsapps.dol.gov/olpdr/Guide_to_Working_with_OLMS_LM_Data.pdf).
verdict.notes: Real and free. The standout feature -- LM-10 employer reports naming employer<->persuader/consultant payments -- is a relationship edge that exists nowhere else, genuinely de-siloing. EIN: LM-2 union annual reports do carry the filer's file number and identifying info and EIN is collected on the forms, so the EIN claim is plausible (likely) but I did not confirm EIN is a populated column in the downloadable extract for all form types -- verify the field on load. Main caveat is OVERLAP: labordata already mirrors these same OLMS tables in cleaner SQLite, so prefer that mirror unless you want the raw authoritative source. Treat as medium, not unique.

candidates.name: HHS-OIG Corporate Integrity Agreements + LEIE Exclusions
candidates.bridges: CIA list names the settling healthcare entity (which carries NPI/CCN in the broader Ripple health backbone) and ties it to a DOJ FCA settlement; LEIE (separate OIG file) carries NPI for excluded providers/entities
candidates.de_silos: connects DOJ FCA settlements (Ripple island) to the dense healthcare NPI/CCN backbone (143+133 edges) and to HHS-OIG exclusions -- 'provider settled fraud, signed a CIA, and/or was excluded' as one chain
candidates.access: CIA list free (browse/filter web + data.gov dataset of ~335 agreements); LEIE downloadable as monthly CSV free at oig.hhs.gov; no auth
candidates.volume: ~335 active CIAs; LEIE ~75k excluded parties
candidates.creative_join: NPI-join CIA/LEIE entities to CMS Open Payments and Medicare CCN data to show 'provider under a corporate integrity agreement still receiving pharma payments / Medicare reimbursement'

verdict.name: HHS-OIG Corporate Integrity Agreements + LEIE Exclusions
verdict.access_reality: Both free, no auth. LEIE: full database + monthly supplement CSVs at oig.hhs.gov/exclusions/download.asp, record layout PDF public. CIA list: browsable/filterable on oig.hhs.gov plus a data.gov dataset (~335 active agreements) -- but the CIA list is essentially entity NAMES + agreement docs, no structured IDs.
verdict.notes: EIN claim is REFUTED: the LEIE record layout (LASTNAME/FIRSTNAME/MIDNAME/BUSNAME/GENERAL/SPECIALTY/UPIN/NPI/DOB/ADDRESS/CITY/STATE/ZIP/EXCLTYPE/EXCLDATE...) has NO EIN field. It carries NPI and UPIN only. NPI is real but SPARSE: OIG only began adding NPI in 2008 and explicitly notes 'many excluded entities do not have NPIs,' so a large share of rows have a blank NPI -- the NPI join into the healthcare backbone works only on the populated subset. The CIA list carries no NPI/EIN at all (name-only), so its only join is fuzzy NAME matching. Still useful for the LEIE NPI bridge, but downgrade the Rosetta-Stone framing -- this is NPI-only-and-sparse, not NPI+EIN.

candidates.name: USDA FSIS Recalls API + Establishments (MPI) Directory
candidates.publisher: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
candidates.bridges: FSIS establishment number joins the Recall API to the MPI Establishment Demographic CSV (company name, owner, address), giving facility->company resolution for food-safety enforcement
candidates.de_silos: creates a food-safety enforcement node that bridges to OSHA/WHD (same meatpacking plant ADDRESS), to USASpending (USDA food-procurement contractors by NAME), and to EPA ECHO (slaughterhouse facilities by ADDRESS/NAICS)
candidates.access: free, no auth; Recall API JSON with attribute querying; Establishment demographic + MPI directory as CSV; daily/real-time
candidates.volume: thousands of recalls (since ~2000) + ~6k FSIS-regulated establishments
candidates.creative_join: ADDRESS+NAME join FSIS meat/poultry plants to OSHA injury citations and WHD wage-theft at the SAME facility -- the recurring meatpacking-abuse story (food safety + labor + immigration) in one establishment key
